items.title: Training a linear classifier
items.scenario: A logistic model has loss L(w, b) on mini-batch data. After forward pass, autograd computes ∇L = (∂L/∂w, ∂L/∂b). Each component says how much L would rise if that parameter increased slightly.
items.explanation: The optimizer subtracts η∇L from (w, b), stepping downhill on the loss surface defined over those parameters.

items.title: Hiking on a topographic map
items.scenario: Elevation h(x, y) on a trail map has gradient ∇h at your GPS position. The arrow perpendicular to contour lines points uphill steepest; walking opposite to ∇h descends fastest locally.
items.explanation: Contour lines are level sets where h is constant; orthogonality of ∇h to contours is the same geometry as loss level sets in 2D parameter slices.

items.title: Feature sensitivity in a vision model
items.scenario: For a scalar output score on an image pixel grid, the gradient ∂score/∂pixel has the same shape as the image. Large-magnitude entries mark pixels that locally most increase the score.
items.explanation: Input gradients are not global explanations, but they quantify local sensitivity—useful for debugging and simple saliency visualizations.
